statements.Name: TwoComponentsNotConnected.Statements.ConnectedRelation.IsConnectedRelation
statements.InlineTexReference: A reachability relation is connected if every pair of vertices is reachable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsConnectedRelation {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  ∀ u v : V, G.Reachable u v

statements.Name: TwoComponentsNotConnected.Statements.TwoComponentsDisconnected.two_components_not_connected
statements.InlineTexReference: Suppose there is a nonempty left class of vertices and a nonempty right class of vertices. If no vertex in the left class can reach any vertex in the right class, then the reachability relation fails to connect all vertices. Therefore the relation is not connected.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom two_components_not_connected {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V)
    (left right : V → Prop) (leftVertex rightVertex : V)
    (left_mem : left leftVertex)
    (right_mem : right rightVertex)
    (separated : ∀ u v : V, left u → right v → ¬ G.Reachable u v) :
    ¬ IsConnectedRelation G

statements.Name: GraphCutVertex.Statements.CutVertexRemoval.cut_vertex_removal_not_connected
statements.Title: Cut Vertex Removal Is Disconnected
statements.InlineTexReference:
If deleting a vertex leaves two nonempty vertex classes with no reachable path between
the classes in the remaining graph, then the vertex-deleted graph is not connected.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom cut_vertex_removal_not_connected {V : Type u} (G : SimpleGraph V) (cutVertex : V)
    (left right : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts → Prop)
    (leftVertex rightVertex : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts)
    (left_mem : left leftVertex)
    (right_mem : right rightVertex)
    (separated_after_removal :
      ∀ u v : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts,
        left u → right v → ¬ ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).coe.Reachable u v) :
    ¬ ∀ u v : ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).verts,
      ((⊤ : G.Subgraph).deleteVerts {cutVertex}).coe.Reachable u v

proofs.Name: GraphCutVertex.Proofs.CutVertexRemoval.cut_vertex_removal_not_connected
proofs.Title: Cut Vertex Removal From Two Separated Classes

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.KColorable
statements.Title: m-colorability
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def KColorable (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  G.Colorable m

statements.Title: Deleted-vertex graph
statements.InlineTexReference:

statements.Title: Vertex separator
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the

statements.Name: FragileMainResult.Statements.Main.T3Conditions
statements.Title: The four extension conditions
statements.InlineTexReference:
The statement package records the graph-theoretic vocabulary used in the
formalization of the main result.  A graph is \(m\)-colorable when it has a
mathlib coloring by the finite palette \(\mathrm{Fin}\,m\).  A set of vertices
is a separator when deleting it leaves two vertices in different connected
components, and a graph is three-connected when it has at least four vertices
and no separator of size at most two.

An \(m\)-fragile graph is one whose three-connected subgraphs are all
\((m-1)\)-colorable.  The predicate \(\mathrm{T3Conditions}\) is the conjunction
of the four coloring-extension conclusions C1--C4 from Theorem 3 of the paper.

The public axioms state Theorem 3, its direct colorability corollary, Theorem 2
in both contrapositive and chromatic-number form, and the final corollary that
graphs with no three-connected subgraph are four-colorable.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def T3Conditions [DecidableEq V] (m : Nat) (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
  (∀ {x y : V}, ¬ G.Adj x y →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m), c x = c y) ∧
  (∀ {x y : V}, x ≠ y →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m), c x ≠ c y) ∧
  (∀ {x y z : V}, x ≠ y → x ≠ z → y ≠ z →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m),
      c x ∉ ({c y, c z} : Finset (Fin m))) ∧
  (∀ {x y z : V}, x ≠ y → x ≠ z → y ≠ z →
    ¬ (G.Adj x y ∧ G.Adj x z ∧ G.Adj y z) →
    ∃ c : G.Coloring (Fin m),
      (({x, y, z} : Finset V).image fun w => c w).card = 2)

statements.Title: Theorem 3
statements.InlineTexReference:

statements.Title: Theorem 2, contrapositive form
statements.InlineTexReference:

statements.Title: Theorem 2
statements.InlineTexReference:

SubmissionData.SubmissionName: Functional Equivalence of Twin-Width and Mixed Minor Number

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Statements.Main.twin_width_functionally_equivalent_mixed_minor_number
statements.Title: Twin-width and mixed minor number are functionally equivalent
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Main theorem.}
For finite simple graphs, the source definitions of twin-width and mixed minor number are functionally equivalent graph parameters.  The proof package discharges this statement using the source theorem chain culminating in \texttt{TwinWidth.MainContract.twin\_width\_functionally\_equivalent\_mixed\_minor\_number}.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_functionally_equivalent_mixed_minor_number :
    TwinWidth.FunctionallyEquivalent
      (fun {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] => TwinWidth.SimpleGraph.twinWidth)
      (fun {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] => TwinWidth.SimpleGraph.mixedMinorNumber)

proofs.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalence.Proofs.Main.twin_width_functionally_equivalent_mixed_minor_number
proofs.Title: Self-contained source proof of functional equivalence

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.bonnet_depres_exponential_gap
statements.Title: Bonnet-Depres exponential twin-width gap
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom bonnet_depres_exponential_gap
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      treewidth (V := V) G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧ 2 ^ k < twinWidth (V := V) G

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponential.Statements.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
statements.Title: Twin-width can be exponential in treewidth
statements.InlineLeanStatement:

    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      treewidth (V := V) G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧ 2 ^ k < twinWidth (V := V) G

SubmissionData.SubmissionName: Twin-Width Can Be Exponential in Treewidth (Self-Contained)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialSelfContained.Statements.Main.GraphParam
statements.Title: Graph parameter
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Compatibility entries.}
The entries \(\texttt{GraphParam}\), \(\texttt{twinWidth}\), and
\(\texttt{treewidth}\) are retained for compatibility with the already-imported
surface of this submission.  They are aliases for the graph-parameter type
\[
  \forall V,\; [\mathrm{Fintype}\ V] \to [\mathrm{DecidableEq}\ V] \to
    \mathrm{SimpleGraph}\ V \to \mathbb{N},
\]
the concrete twin-width parameter from the vendored source, and the concrete
treewidth parameter from the vendored source.

The compatibility theorem states that for every natural number \(k\), there is
a finite type \(V\), decidable equality on \(V\), and a simple graph \(G\) on
\(V\) such that
\[
  \operatorname{tw}(G) \le 2k+4
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  2^k < \operatorname{tww}(G).
\]

\paragraph{Expanded statement.}
The new expanded theorem states the same Bonnet--Depres separation but spells
out both graph parameters in the Lean statement itself.  It expands treewidth
directly as the natural-number minimum, implemented by \(\texttt{Nat.find}\),
of all widths \(w\) for which \(G\) has a finite tree decomposition whose
largest bag has size at most \(w+1\).  Equivalently, a witness is a tree
decomposition \(D\) with
\[
  \Bigl(\max_i |D_i|\Bigr)-1 \le w .
\]

The Lean statement also expands twin-width directly as the natural-number
minimum, again implemented by \(\texttt{Nat.find}\), of all red-degree bounds \(d\) for
which \(G\) has a contraction sequence of width at most \(d\).  Thus the
second inequality says that every contraction sequence of the graph has width
strictly greater than \(2^k\).

\paragraph{Proof content.}
The proof instantiates \(G\) with the explicit Bonnet--Depres graph
\(BD_k\).  The submitted proof then uses the proved tree-decomposition bound
\(\operatorname{tw}(BD_k) \le 2k+4\) and the proved lower bound
\(\operatorname{tww}(BD_k) > 2^k\).  Both the compatibility theorem and the
expanded theorem are proved directly from these source lemmas.  No contract
axiom supplies either result: the lower bound is proved from the
Bonnet--Depres construction in the vendored source files.
statements.InlineLeanStatement: def GraphParam := TwinWidth.GraphParam

statements.Title: Twin-width can be exponential in treewidth
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Compatibility entries.}
The entries \(\texttt{GraphParam}\), \(\texttt{twinWidth}\), and
\(\texttt{treewidth}\) are retained for compatibility with the already-imported
surface of this submission.  They are aliases for the graph-parameter type
\[
  \forall V,\; [\mathrm{Fintype}\ V] \to [\mathrm{DecidableEq}\ V] \to
    \mathrm{SimpleGraph}\ V \to \mathbb{N},
\]
the concrete twin-width parameter from the vendored source, and the concrete
treewidth parameter from the vendored source.

The compatibility theorem states that for every natural number \(k\), there is
a finite type \(V\), decidable equality on \(V\), and a simple graph \(G\) on
\(V\) such that
\[
  \operatorname{tw}(G) \le 2k+4
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  2^k < \operatorname{tww}(G).
\]

\paragraph{Expanded statement.}
The new expanded theorem states the same Bonnet--Depres separation but spells
out both graph parameters in the Lean statement itself.  It expands treewidth
directly as the natural-number minimum, implemented by \(\texttt{Nat.find}\),
of all widths \(w\) for which \(G\) has a finite tree decomposition whose
largest bag has size at most \(w+1\).  Equivalently, a witness is a tree
decomposition \(D\) with
\[
  \Bigl(\max_i |D_i|\Bigr)-1 \le w .
\]

The Lean statement also expands twin-width directly as the natural-number
minimum, again implemented by \(\texttt{Nat.find}\), of all red-degree bounds \(d\) for
which \(G\) has a contraction sequence of width at most \(d\).  Thus the
second inequality says that every contraction sequence of the graph has width
strictly greater than \(2^k\).

\paragraph{Proof content.}
The proof instantiates \(G\) with the explicit Bonnet--Depres graph
\(BD_k\).  The submitted proof then uses the proved tree-decomposition bound
\(\operatorname{tw}(BD_k) \le 2k+4\) and the proved lower bound
\(\operatorname{tww}(BD_k) > 2^k\).  Both the compatibility theorem and the
expanded theorem are proved directly from these source lemmas.  No contract
axiom supplies either result: the lower bound is proved from the
Bonnet--Depres construction in the vendored source files.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      treewidth (V := V) G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧ 2 ^ k < twinWidth (V := V) G

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialSelfContained.Statements.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth_expanded
statements.Title: Twin-width can be exponential in treewidth, expanded form
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Compatibility entries.}
The entries \(\texttt{GraphParam}\), \(\texttt{twinWidth}\), and
\(\texttt{treewidth}\) are retained for compatibility with the already-imported
surface of this submission.  They are aliases for the graph-parameter type
\[
  \forall V,\; [\mathrm{Fintype}\ V] \to [\mathrm{DecidableEq}\ V] \to
    \mathrm{SimpleGraph}\ V \to \mathbb{N},
\]
the concrete twin-width parameter from the vendored source, and the concrete
treewidth parameter from the vendored source.

The compatibility theorem states that for every natural number \(k\), there is
a finite type \(V\), decidable equality on \(V\), and a simple graph \(G\) on
\(V\) such that
\[
  \operatorname{tw}(G) \le 2k+4
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  2^k < \operatorname{tww}(G).
\]

\paragraph{Expanded statement.}
The new expanded theorem states the same Bonnet--Depres separation but spells
out both graph parameters in the Lean statement itself.  It expands treewidth
directly as the natural-number minimum, implemented by \(\texttt{Nat.find}\),
of all widths \(w\) for which \(G\) has a finite tree decomposition whose
largest bag has size at most \(w+1\).  Equivalently, a witness is a tree
decomposition \(D\) with
\[
  \Bigl(\max_i |D_i|\Bigr)-1 \le w .
\]

The Lean statement also expands twin-width directly as the natural-number
minimum, again implemented by \(\texttt{Nat.find}\), of all red-degree bounds \(d\) for
which \(G\) has a contraction sequence of width at most \(d\).  Thus the
second inequality says that every contraction sequence of the graph has width
strictly greater than \(2^k\).

\paragraph{Proof content.}
The proof instantiates \(G\) with the explicit Bonnet--Depres graph
\(BD_k\).  The submitted proof then uses the proved tree-decomposition bound
\(\operatorname{tw}(BD_k) \le 2k+4\) and the proved lower bound
\(\operatorname{tww}(BD_k) > 2^k\).  Both the compatibility theorem and the
expanded theorem are proved directly from these source lemmas.  No contract
axiom supplies either result: the lower bound is proved from the
Bonnet--Depres construction in the vendored source files.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth_expanded
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      (by
        classical
        exact
          if htw :
              ∃ width : Nat,
                ∃ D : TwinWidth.SimpleGraph.TreeDecomposition G,
                  (letI : Fintype D.Node := D.nodeFintype;
                    (Finset.univ.sup fun i : D.Node => (D.bag i).card) - 1) ≤ width
            then Nat.find htw else 0) ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧
        2 ^ k <
          (by
            classical
            exact
              if htww :
                  ∃ d : Nat,
                    Nonempty (TwinWidth.SimpleGraph.ContractionSequence G d)
                then Nat.find htww else 0)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialTransparent.Statements.Main.bonnet_depres_tree_decomposition_and_no_small_contraction_sequence
statements.Title: Bonnet-Depres graph has small tree decomposition and no small contraction sequence
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Statement.}
For every natural number \(k\), there is a finite type \(V\), decidable equality
on \(V\), a simple graph \(G\) on \(V\), and a tree decomposition \(D\) of \(G\)
such that
\[
  \Bigl(\max_i |D_i|\Bigr)-1 \le 2k+4 .
\]
In addition, \(G\) has no contraction sequence whose red degree is bounded by
\(2^k\).  The Lean statement says this directly as
\[
  \neg\,\mathrm{Nonempty}
    (\mathrm{ContractionSequence}\ G\ (2^k)).
\]
Thus the submitted entry does not introduce or submit aliases for graph
parameters, treewidth, or twin-width.  The treewidth side is witnessed by the
explicit decomposition \(D\), and the twin-width side is phrased as the
nonexistence of a small contraction sequence.

\paragraph{Proof content.}
The proof instantiates \(G\) with the explicit Bonnet--Depres graph
\(BD_k\) and \(D\) with its explicit Bonnet--Depres tree decomposition.  The
submitted proof uses the proved bound on the maximum bag size of this
decomposition and the proved theorem that no \(2^k\)-bounded contraction
sequence exists for \(BD_k\).  No contract axiom supplies the result: both
facts are proved from the Bonnet--Depres construction in the vendored source
files.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom bonnet_depres_tree_decomposition_and_no_small_contraction_sequence
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V) (D : TwinWidth.SimpleGraph.TreeDecomposition G),
      (letI : Fintype D.Node := D.nodeFintype;
        (Finset.univ.sup fun i : D.Node => (D.bag i).card) - 1) ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧
        ¬ Nonempty (TwinWidth.SimpleGraph.ContractionSequence G (2 ^ k))

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.SingletonBags.singletonBags
statements.Title: Singleton bag partition
statements.InlineTexReference:
For a finite vertex type \(V\), \(\texttt{singletonBags}(V)\) is the family
\[
  \{\{v\}: v\in V\}.
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def singletonBags (V : Type) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] : Finset (Finset V) :=
  Finset.univ.image (fun v : V => ({v} : Finset V))

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.RedDegree.redDegree
statements.Title: Red degree in a trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
Given a finite set \(B\) of current bags and a red adjacency relation \(R\) on
bags, \(\texttt{redDegree}(B,R,A)\) is
\[
  |\{C\in B : R(A,C)\}|.
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def redDegree {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (bags : Finset (Finset V)) (redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop)
    (A : Finset V) : ℕ := by
  classical
  exact (bags.filter fun B => redAdj A B).card

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.TrigraphState.IsTrigraphState
statements.Title: Trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{IsTrigraphState}(B,E_{\mathrm{black}},E_{\mathrm{red}})\) says that
\(B\) is a partition of the vertex type into nonempty finite bags, the black and
red relations are symmetric on bags, neither relation has a loop, and no pair of
bags is both black-adjacent and red-adjacent.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsTrigraphState {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (bags : Finset (Finset V))
    (blackAdj redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop) : Prop :=
  (∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags → A.Nonempty) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → A ≠ B → Disjoint A B) ∧
  (∀ v : V, ∃ A ∈ bags, v ∈ A) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → blackAdj A B → blackAdj B A) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → redAdj A B → redAdj B A) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags → ¬ blackAdj A A) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags → ¬ redAdj A A) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → blackAdj A B → ¬ redAdj A B)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.InitialTrigraphState.IsInitialTrigraphState
statements.Title: Initial trigraph state of a graph
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{IsInitialTrigraphState}(G,B,E_{\mathrm{black}},E_{\mathrm{red}})\)
says that \(B=\{\{v\}:v\in V(G)\}\), black adjacency between two bags means
there is an edge of \(G\) between a vertex in the first bag and a vertex in the
second bag, and there are no red adjacencies.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsInitialTrigraphState {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) (bags : Finset (Finset V))
    (blackAdj redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop) : Prop :=
  bags = TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.SingletonBags.singletonBags V ∧
  (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags →
    (blackAdj A B ↔ ∃ a ∈ A, ∃ b ∈ B, G.Adj a b)) ∧
  (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → ¬ redAdj A B)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.FinalTrigraphState.IsFinalTrigraphState
statements.Title: Final trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{IsFinalTrigraphState}(B)\) says that the current family of bags has
cardinality at most one:
\[
  |B|\le 1.
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsFinalTrigraphState {V : Type} (bags : Finset (Finset V)) : Prop :=
  bags.card ≤ 1

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractedRed.contractedRed
statements.Title: Red adjacency after contraction
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{contractedRed}(E_{\mathrm{black}},E_{\mathrm{red}},A,B,X,Y)\) is
the red adjacency after contracting \(A\) and \(B\).  Loops are not red.  For
the merged bag \(A\cup B\), red adjacency to another bag \(Y\) is inherited
from either old red adjacency \(E_{\mathrm{red}}(A,Y)\) or
\(E_{\mathrm{red}}(B,Y)\), or is created when the old black adjacencies
\(E_{\mathrm{black}}(A,Y)\) and \(E_{\mathrm{black}}(B,Y)\) disagree.  Pairs
not involving the merged bag keep their old red adjacency.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def contractedRed {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (blackAdj redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop)
    (A B X Y : Finset V) : Prop :=
  if X = Y then
    False
  else if X = A ∪ B then
    redAdj A Y ∨ redAdj B Y ∨ blackAdj A Y ≠ blackAdj B Y
  else if Y = A ∪ B then
    redAdj X A ∨ redAdj X B ∨ blackAdj X A ≠ blackAdj X B
  else
    redAdj X Y

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractedBlack.contractedBlack
statements.Title: Black adjacency after contraction
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{contractedBlack}(E_{\mathrm{black}},E_{\mathrm{red}},A,B,X,Y)\) is
the black adjacency after contracting \(A\) and \(B\).  Loops are not black.
For the merged bag \(A\cup B\), black adjacency to \(Y\) requires both old black
adjacencies to \(Y\) and requires that no red adjacency is created by
\(\texttt{contractedRed}\).  Pairs not involving the merged bag keep their old
black adjacency.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def contractedBlack {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (blackAdj redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop)
    (A B X Y : Finset V) : Prop :=
  if X = Y then
    False
  else if X = A ∪ B then
    blackAdj A Y ∧ blackAdj B Y ∧
      ¬ TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractedRed.contractedRed
        blackAdj redAdj A B X Y
  else if Y = A ∪ B then
    blackAdj X A ∧ blackAdj X B ∧
      ¬ TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractedRed.contractedRed
        blackAdj redAdj A B X Y
  else
    blackAdj X Y

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractionStep.IsContractionStep
statements.Title: One contraction step
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{IsContractionStep}\) says that one transition replaces two distinct
current bags \(A,B\) by \(A\cup B\):
\[
  B_{\mathrm{next}}=\{A\cup B\}\cup (B_{\mathrm{old}}\setminus\{A,B\}),
\]
and that the next red and black adjacencies are exactly the relations defined
by \(\texttt{contractedRed}\) and \(\texttt{contractedBlack}\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def IsContractionStep {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (bags : Finset (Finset V))
    (blackAdj redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop)
    (nextBags : Finset (Finset V))
    (nextBlackAdj nextRedAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop) : Prop :=
  ∃ A ∈ bags, ∃ B ∈ bags, A ≠ B ∧
    nextBags = insert (A ∪ B) ((bags.erase A).erase B) ∧
    (∀ ⦃X Y⦄, X ∈ nextBags → Y ∈ nextBags →
      (nextRedAdj X Y ↔
        TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractedRed.contractedRed
          blackAdj redAdj A B X Y)) ∧
    (∀ ⦃X Y⦄, X ∈ nextBags → Y ∈ nextBags →
      (nextBlackAdj X Y ↔
        TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractedBlack.contractedBlack
          blackAdj redAdj A B X Y))

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractionSequenceWidth.HasContractionSequenceWidthAtMost
statements.Title: Bounded-width contraction sequence
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{HasContractionSequenceWidthAtMost}(G,d)\) says that there is a finite
sequence of trigraph states starting from the singleton-bag trigraph of \(G\),
ending with at most one bag, advancing by one contraction step at each time, and
with red degree at most \(d\) for every bag in every state up to the final
state.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def HasContractionSequenceWidthAtMost {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) (d : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∃ stepCount : ℕ,
  ∃ bags : ℕ → Finset (Finset V),
  ∃ blackAdj : ℕ → Finset V → Finset V → Prop,
  ∃ redAdj : ℕ → Finset V → Finset V → Prop,
    (∀ i,
      TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.TrigraphState.IsTrigraphState
        (bags i) (blackAdj i) (redAdj i)) ∧
    TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.InitialTrigraphState.IsInitialTrigraphState G
      (bags 0) (blackAdj 0) (redAdj 0) ∧
    TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.FinalTrigraphState.IsFinalTrigraphState
      (bags stepCount) ∧
    (∀ i, i < stepCount →
      TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractionStep.IsContractionStep
        (bags i) (blackAdj i) (redAdj i)
        (bags (i + 1)) (blackAdj (i + 1)) (redAdj (i + 1))) ∧
    (∀ i, i ≤ stepCount → ∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags i →
      TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.RedDegree.redDegree
        (bags i) (redAdj i) A ≤ d)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.HasTreeDecompositionWidthAtMost
statements.Title: Bounded-width tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{HasTreeDecompositionWidthAtMost}(G,w)\) says that there is a finite
tree \(T\) and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for \(t\in V(T)\) such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\), and
\[
  \max_t |X_t|-1 \le w .
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def HasTreeDecompositionWidthAtMost {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) (width : ℕ) : Prop :=
  ∃ Node : Type,
  ∃ nodeFintype : Fintype Node,
  ∃ _nodeDecidableEq : DecidableEq Node,
  ∃ tree : SimpleGraph Node,
    tree.IsTree ∧
    ∃ bag : Node → Finset V,
      (∀ v : V, ∃ i : Node, v ∈ bag i) ∧
      (∀ ⦃u v : V⦄, G.Adj u v → ∃ i : Node, u ∈ bag i ∧ v ∈ bag i) ∧
      (∀ v : V, (tree.induce {i : Node | v ∈ bag i}).Connected) ∧
      (letI : Fintype Node := nodeFintype;
        (Finset.univ.sup fun i : Node => (bag i).card) - 1) ≤ width

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.Treewidth.treewidth
statements.Title: Treewidth
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{treewidth}(G)\) is the least natural number \(w\) such that
\(\texttt{HasTreeDecompositionWidthAtMost}(G,w)\).  In Lean this least value is
implemented with \(\texttt{Nat.find}\), with fallback value \(0\) in the empty
search branch.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def treewidth {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ := by
  classical
  exact if h : ∃ width,
      TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.HasTreeDecompositionWidthAtMost
        G width
    then Nat.find h else 0

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.TwinWidth.twinWidth
statements.Title: Twin-width
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\texttt{twinWidth}(G)\) is the least natural number \(d\) such that
\(\texttt{HasContractionSequenceWidthAtMost}(G,d)\).  In Lean this least value
is implemented with \(\texttt{Nat.find}\), with fallback value \(0\) in the
empty search branch.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def twinWidth {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ := by
  classical
  exact if h : ∃ d,
      TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.ContractionSequenceWidth.HasContractionSequenceWidthAtMost
        G d
    then Nat.find h else 0

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
statements.Title: Twin-width can be exponential in treewidth
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Statement.}
For every \(k\in\mathbb{N}\), there is a finite type \(V\), decidable equality
on \(V\), and a simple graph \(G\) on \(V\) such that
\[
  \texttt{treewidth}(G) \le 2k+4
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  2^k < \texttt{twinWidth}(G).
\]

\paragraph{Proof content.}
The proof uses the explicit Bonnet--Depres graph \(BD_k\) from the submitted
source files.  It translates the proved Bonnet--Depres tree decomposition into
the submitted tree-decomposition-width predicate, and translates any submitted
bounded contraction sequence back into the source contraction-sequence
structure.  The source proof that \(BD_k\) has no \(2^k\)-bounded contraction
sequence therefore proves the submitted lower bound on \(\texttt{twinWidth}\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.Treewidth.treewidth G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧
        2 ^ k < TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption.Statements.TwinWidth.twinWidth G

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.LeastNatural.leastNat
statements.Title: Least natural number satisfying a predicate
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{leastNat}(P)\) is the least natural number satisfying the
predicate \(P\), when such a number exists.  If no natural number satisfies
\(P\), it is defined to be \(0\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def leastNat (P : ℕ → Prop) : ℕ :=
  letI : Decidable (∃ n, P n) := Classical.propDecidable _
  letI : DecidablePred P := Classical.decPred P
  if h : ∃ n, P n then Nat.find h else 0

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.SingletonBags.singletonBags
statements.Title: Singleton bag partition
statements.InlineTexReference:
For a finite vertex type \(V\), \(\mathrm{singletonBags}(V)\) is the family
\[
  \{\{v\}: v\in V\}.
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def singletonBags (V : Type) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] : Finset (Finset V) :=
  Finset.univ.image (fun v : V => ({v} : Finset V))

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TrigraphState.State
statements.Title: Trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{State}(V)\) is the type of trigraph states on the original
vertex type \(V\).  An element stores a finite family of current bags, a black
adjacency relation on bags, and a red adjacency relation on bags.  The stored
invariants say that the bags are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and cover \(V\);
both adjacency relations are symmetric on current bags and loopless; and no
pair of current bags is both black-adjacent and red-adjacent.

The accompanying definitions \(\mathrm{bags}(T)\),
\(\mathrm{blackAdj}(T)\), and \(\mathrm{redAdj}(T)\) return the
three data components of a state \(T:\mathrm{State}(V)\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def State (V : Type) [DecidableEq V] : Type :=
  Σ bags : Finset (Finset V),
  Σ blackAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop,
  Σ redAdj : Finset V → Finset V → Prop,
    PLift (
    (∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags → A.Nonempty) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → A ≠ B → Disjoint A B) ∧
    (∀ v : V, ∃ A ∈ bags, v ∈ A) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → blackAdj A B → blackAdj B A) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → redAdj A B → redAdj B A) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags → ¬ blackAdj A A) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags → ¬ redAdj A A) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags → B ∈ bags → blackAdj A B → ¬ redAdj A B))

/-- The current bags of a trigraph state. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TrigraphState.bags
statements.Title: Bags of a trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{State}(V)\) is the type of trigraph states on the original
vertex type \(V\).  An element stores a finite family of current bags, a black
adjacency relation on bags, and a red adjacency relation on bags.  The stored
invariants say that the bags are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and cover \(V\);
both adjacency relations are symmetric on current bags and loopless; and no
pair of current bags is both black-adjacent and red-adjacent.

The accompanying definitions \(\mathrm{bags}(T)\),
\(\mathrm{blackAdj}(T)\), and \(\mathrm{redAdj}(T)\) return the
three data components of a state \(T:\mathrm{State}(V)\).

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TrigraphState.blackAdj
statements.Title: Black adjacency of a trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{State}(V)\) is the type of trigraph states on the original
vertex type \(V\).  An element stores a finite family of current bags, a black
adjacency relation on bags, and a red adjacency relation on bags.  The stored
invariants say that the bags are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and cover \(V\);
both adjacency relations are symmetric on current bags and loopless; and no
pair of current bags is both black-adjacent and red-adjacent.

The accompanying definitions \(\mathrm{bags}(T)\),
\(\mathrm{blackAdj}(T)\), and \(\mathrm{redAdj}(T)\) return the
three data components of a state \(T:\mathrm{State}(V)\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def blackAdj {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] (T : State V) :
    Finset V → Finset V → Prop :=
  Sigma.fst (Sigma.snd T)

/-- The red adjacency relation of a trigraph state. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TrigraphState.redAdj
statements.Title: Red adjacency of a trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{State}(V)\) is the type of trigraph states on the original
vertex type \(V\).  An element stores a finite family of current bags, a black
adjacency relation on bags, and a red adjacency relation on bags.  The stored
invariants say that the bags are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, and cover \(V\);
both adjacency relations are symmetric on current bags and loopless; and no
pair of current bags is both black-adjacent and red-adjacent.

The accompanying definitions \(\mathrm{bags}(T)\),
\(\mathrm{blackAdj}(T)\), and \(\mathrm{redAdj}(T)\) return the
three data components of a state \(T:\mathrm{State}(V)\).

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.RedDegree.redDegree
statements.Title: Red degree in a trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
For a trigraph state \(T\) and a current bag \(A\),
\(\mathrm{redDegree}(T,A)\) is
\[
  |\{C\in\mathrm{bags}(T) : \mathrm{redAdj}(T,A,C)\}|.
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def redDegree {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (T : State V) (A : Finset V) : ℕ :=
  letI : DecidablePred (fun B => redAdj T A B) :=
    Classical.decPred (fun B => redAdj T A B)
  ((bags T).filter fun B => redAdj T A B).card

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.InitialTrigraphState.InitialState
statements.Title: Initial trigraph state of a graph
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{InitialState}(G)\) is the subtype of trigraph states whose bags
are the singleton bags
\[
  \{\{v\}:v\in V(G)\},
\]
whose black adjacency between two bags means that there is an edge of \(G\)
between a vertex in the first bag and a vertex in the second bag, and whose red
adjacency relation is empty.

For \(I:\mathrm{InitialState}(G)\), \(\mathrm{state}(I)\) is its
underlying trigraph state.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def InitialState {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) : Type :=
  Σ state : State V,
    PLift (
    bags state = singletonBags V ∧
    (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags state → B ∈ bags state →
      (blackAdj state A B ↔ ∃ a ∈ A, ∃ b ∈ B, G.Adj a b)) ∧
    (∀ ⦃A B⦄, A ∈ bags state → B ∈ bags state →
      ¬ redAdj state A B))

/-- The underlying trigraph state of an initial state. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.InitialTrigraphState.state
statements.Title: Underlying state of an initial trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{InitialState}(G)\) is the subtype of trigraph states whose bags
are the singleton bags
\[
  \{\{v\}:v\in V(G)\},
\]
whose black adjacency between two bags means that there is an edge of \(G\)
between a vertex in the first bag and a vertex in the second bag, and whose red
adjacency relation is empty.

For \(I:\mathrm{InitialState}(G)\), \(\mathrm{state}(I)\) is its
underlying trigraph state.

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.FinalTrigraphState.FinalState
statements.Title: Final trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{FinalState}(V)\) is the subtype of trigraph states on \(V\)
whose current family of bags has cardinality at most one:
\[
  |\mathrm{bags}(T)|\le 1.
\]

For \(F:\mathrm{FinalState}(V)\), \(\mathrm{state}(F)\) is its
underlying trigraph state.

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.FinalTrigraphState.state
statements.Title: Underlying state of a final trigraph state
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{FinalState}(V)\) is the subtype of trigraph states on \(V\)
whose current family of bags has cardinality at most one:
\[
  |\mathrm{bags}(T)|\le 1.
\]

For \(F:\mathrm{FinalState}(V)\), \(\mathrm{state}(F)\) is its
underlying trigraph state.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def state {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] (F : FinalState V) : State V :=
  Sigma.fst F

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.ContractedRed.contractedRed
statements.Title: Red adjacency after contraction
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{contractedRed}(T,A,B,X,Y)\) is the red adjacency relation
defined from a trigraph state \(T\) after contracting the two bags \(A\) and
\(B\).  Loops are not red.  For the merged bag \(A\cup B\), red adjacency to
another bag \(Y\) is inherited from either old red adjacency
\(R_T(A,Y)\) or \(R_T(B,Y)\), or is created when the old black adjacencies
\(K_T(A,Y)\) and \(K_T(B,Y)\) disagree.  Pairs not involving the merged bag
keep their old red adjacency in \(T\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def contractedRed {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (T : State V) (A B X Y : Finset V) : Prop :=
  if X = Y then
    False
  else if X = A ∪ B then
    redAdj T A Y ∨ redAdj T B Y ∨
      blackAdj T A Y ≠ blackAdj T B Y
  else if Y = A ∪ B then
    redAdj T X A ∨ redAdj T X B ∨
      blackAdj T X A ≠ blackAdj T X B
  else
    redAdj T X Y

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.ContractedBlack.contractedBlack
statements.Title: Black adjacency after contraction
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{contractedBlack}(T,A,B,X,Y)\) is the black adjacency relation
defined from a trigraph state \(T\) after contracting the two bags \(A\) and
\(B\).  Loops are not black.  For the merged bag \(A\cup B\), black adjacency
to \(Y\) requires both old black adjacencies to \(Y\) and requires that
\(\mathrm{contractedRed}(T,A,B,X,Y)\) is false.  Pairs not involving the
merged bag keep their old black adjacency in \(T\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def contractedBlack {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (T : State V) (A B X Y : Finset V) : Prop :=
  if X = Y then
    False
  else if X = A ∪ B then
    blackAdj T A Y ∧ blackAdj T B Y ∧
      ¬ contractedRed T A B X Y
  else if Y = A ∪ B then
    blackAdj T X A ∧ blackAdj T X B ∧
      ¬ contractedRed T A B X Y
  else
    blackAdj T X Y

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.ContractionStep.Step
statements.Title: One contraction step
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{Step}(T,U)\) is the type of witnesses that the trigraph state
\(U\) is obtained from the trigraph state \(T\) by one contraction.  Such a
witness chooses two distinct current bags \(A,B\) of \(T\) and states that they
are replaced by \(A\cup B\):
\[
  \mathrm{bags}(U)=\{A\cup B\}\cup
    (\mathrm{bags}(T)\setminus\{A,B\}),
\]
and that the red and black adjacencies of \(U\) are exactly the relations
defined by \(\mathrm{contractedRed}(T,A,B,-,-)\) and
\(\mathrm{contractedBlack}(T,A,B,-,-)\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def Step {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (T U : State V) : Type :=
  Σ left : Finset V,
  Σ right : Finset V,
    PLift (
    left ∈ bags T ∧
    right ∈ bags T ∧
    left ≠ right ∧
    bags U = insert (left ∪ right) (((bags T).erase left).erase right) ∧
    (∀ ⦃X Y⦄, X ∈ bags U → Y ∈ bags U →
      (redAdj U X Y ↔ contractedRed T left right X Y)) ∧
    (∀ ⦃X Y⦄, X ∈ bags U → Y ∈ bags U →
      (blackAdj U X Y ↔ contractedBlack T left right X Y)))

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.ContractionSequenceWidth.ContractionSequence
statements.Title: Bounded-width contraction sequence
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{ContractionSequence}(G,d)\) is the type of bounded contraction
sequences of the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite time horizon,
a trigraph state at each time, a witness that time \(0\) is the initial
singleton-bag state of \(G\), a witness that the final time has at most one bag,
a contraction-step witness between every successive pair of states, and the
bound
\[
  \mathrm{redDegree}(T_i,A)\le d
\]
for every time \(i\) up to the final time and every current bag \(A\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def ContractionSequence {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) (d : ℕ) : Type :=
  Σ stepCount : ℕ,
  Σ state : ℕ → State V,
  Σ start : InitialState G,
  Σ final : FinalState V,
    PLift (state 0 = InitialTrigraphState.state start) ×
    PLift (state stepCount = FinalTrigraphState.state final) ×
    (∀ i, i < stepCount → Step (state i) (state (i + 1))) ×
    PLift (∀ i, i ≤ stepCount → ∀ ⦃A⦄, A ∈ bags (state i) →
      redDegree (state i) A ≤ d)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.TreeDecomposition
statements.Title: Tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\).  For such a decomposition \(D\),
\(\mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\) is
\[
  \max_t |X_t|-1 .
\]
The auxiliary definitions \(\mathrm{nodeType}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{nodeFintype}(D)\), \(\mathrm{nodeDecidableEq}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{tree}(D)\), and \(\mathrm{bag}(D)\) return the displayed
components of \(D\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def TreeDecomposition {V : Type} [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) : Type 1 :=
  Σ Node : Type,
  Σ _nodeFintype : Fintype Node,
  Σ _nodeDecidableEq : DecidableEq Node,
  Σ tree : SimpleGraph Node,
  Σ bag : Node → Finset V,
    PLift (
    tree.IsTree ∧
    (∀ v : V, ∃ i : Node, v ∈ bag i) ∧
    (∀ ⦃u v : V⦄, G.Adj u v → ∃ i : Node, u ∈ bag i ∧ v ∈ bag i) ∧
    (∀ v : V, (tree.induce {i : Node | v ∈ bag i}).Connected))

/-- The node type of a tree decomposition. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.nodeType
statements.Title: Node type of a tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\).  For such a decomposition \(D\),
\(\mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\) is
\[

\]
The auxiliary definitions \(\mathrm{nodeType}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{nodeFintype}(D)\), \(\mathrm{nodeDecidableEq}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{tree}(D)\), and \(\mathrm{bag}(D)\) return the displayed
components of \(D\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def nodeType {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] {G : SimpleGraph V}
    (D : TreeDecomposition G) : Type :=
  Sigma.fst D

/-- The finiteness witness for the node type of a tree decomposition. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.nodeFintype
statements.Title: Finiteness of the node type of a tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is

statements.InlineLeanStatement:
@[reducible]
def nodeFintype {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] {G : SimpleGraph V}
    (D : TreeDecomposition G) : Fintype (nodeType D) :=
  Sigma.fst (Sigma.snd D)

/-- The decidable-equality witness for the node type of a tree decomposition. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.nodeDecidableEq
statements.Title: Decidable equality on the node type of a tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\).  For such a decomposition \(D\),
\(\mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\) is
\[
  \max_t |X_t|-1 .
\]
The auxiliary definitions \(\mathrm{nodeType}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{nodeFintype}(D)\), \(\mathrm{nodeDecidableEq}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{tree}(D)\), and \(\mathrm{bag}(D)\) return the displayed
components of \(D\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
@[reducible]
def nodeDecidableEq {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] {G : SimpleGraph V}
    (D : TreeDecomposition G) : DecidableEq (nodeType D) :=
  Sigma.fst (Sigma.snd (Sigma.snd D))

/-- The decomposition tree. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.tree
statements.Title: Tree of a tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\).  For such a decomposition \(D\),
\(\mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\) is
\[
  \max_t |X_t|-1 .
\]
The auxiliary definitions \(\mathrm{nodeType}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{nodeFintype}(D)\), \(\mathrm{nodeDecidableEq}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{tree}(D)\), and \(\mathrm{bag}(D)\) return the displayed
components of \(D\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def tree {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] {G : SimpleGraph V}
    (D : TreeDecomposition G) : SimpleGraph (nodeType D) :=
  Sigma.fst (Sigma.snd (Sigma.snd (Sigma.snd D)))

/-- The bag assigned to each decomposition node. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.bag
statements.Title: Bags of a tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\).  For such a decomposition \(D\),
\(\mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\) is
\[
  \max_t |X_t|-1 .
\]
The auxiliary definitions \(\mathrm{nodeType}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{nodeFintype}(D)\), \(\mathrm{nodeDecidableEq}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{tree}(D)\), and \(\mathrm{bag}(D)\) return the displayed
components of \(D\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def bag {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] {G : SimpleGraph V}
    (D : TreeDecomposition G) : nodeType D → Finset V :=
  Sigma.fst (Sigma.snd (Sigma.snd (Sigma.snd (Sigma.snd D))))

/-- The width of a tree decomposition: maximum bag size minus one. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TreeDecompositionWidth.treeDecompositionWidth
statements.Title: Width of a tree decomposition
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) is the type of tree decompositions of
the graph \(G\).  An element consists of a finite node type, a tree \(T\) on
that node type, and bags \(X_t\subseteq V(G)\) for its nodes, such that every
vertex of \(G\) appears in some bag, every edge of \(G\) has both endpoints in
some bag, and the set of tree nodes whose bags contain any fixed vertex is
connected in \(T\).  For such a decomposition \(D\),
\(\mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\) is
\[
  \max_t |X_t|-1 .
\]
The auxiliary definitions \(\mathrm{nodeType}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{nodeFintype}(D)\), \(\mathrm{nodeDecidableEq}(D)\),
\(\mathrm{tree}(D)\), and \(\mathrm{bag}(D)\) return the displayed
components of \(D\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
noncomputable def treeDecompositionWidth
    {V : Type} [DecidableEq V] {G : SimpleGraph V}
    (D : TreeDecomposition G) : ℕ :=
  letI : Fintype (nodeType D) := nodeFintype D
  (Finset.univ.sup fun i : nodeType D => (bag D i).card) - 1

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.Treewidth.treewidth
statements.Title: Treewidth
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{treewidth}(G)\) is the least natural number \(w\) such that
there exists \(D:\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G)\) with
\[
  \mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\le w.
\]
Equivalently, it is
\[
  \mathrm{leastNat}\bigl(w\mapsto
    \exists D:\mathrm{TreeDecomposition}(G),\,
      \mathrm{treeDecompositionWidth}(D)\le w\bigr).
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def treewidth {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ :=
  leastNat fun width => ∃ D : TreeDecomposition G,
    treeDecompositionWidth D ≤ width

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.TwinWidth.twinWidth
statements.Title: Twin-width
statements.InlineTexReference:
\(\mathrm{twinWidth}(G)\) is the least natural number \(d\) such that
\(\mathrm{ContractionSequence}(G,d)\) is nonempty:
\[
  \mathrm{leastNat}\bigl(d\mapsto
    \mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathrm{ContractionSequence}(G,d))\bigr).
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def twinWidth {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ :=
  leastNat fun d => Nonempty (ContractionSequence G d)

statements.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Statements.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
statements.Title: Twin-width can be exponential in treewidth
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Statement.}
For every \(k\in\mathbb{N}\), there is a finite type \(V\), decidable equality
on \(V\), and a simple graph \(G\) on \(V\) such that
\[
  \mathrm{treewidth}(G) \le 2k+4
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  2^k < \mathrm{twinWidth}(G).
\]

\paragraph{Proof content.}
The proof uses the explicit Bonnet--Depres graph \(BD_k\) from the submitted
source files.  It translates the proved Bonnet--Depres tree decomposition into
the submitted tree-decomposition-width predicate, and translates any submitted
bounded contraction sequence back into the source contraction-sequence
structure.  The source proof that \(BD_k\) has no \(2^k\)-bounded contraction
sequence therefore proves the submitted lower bound on
\(\mathrm{twinWidth}\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
    (k : Nat) :
    ∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V)
      (G : SimpleGraph V),
      treewidth G ≤ 2 * k + 4 ∧ 2 ^ k < twinWidth G

proofs.Name: TwinWidthTreewidthExponentialRedemption2.Proofs.Main.twin_width_can_be_exponential_in_treewidth
proofs.Title: Self-contained proof of the Bonnet-Depres exponential gap

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.IntervalDivision.Division
statements.Title: Division of a finite interval into consecutive parts
statements.InlineTexReference:
For natural numbers \(n\) and \(k\), an interval division
\(\mathrm{Division}(n,k)\) is a family of \(k\) finite subsets of
\(\{0,\ldots,n-1\}\).  The parts are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, cover the
whole interval, are convex in the natural order, and are ordered by their
indices: if \(i<j\), every element of the \(i\)-th part is smaller than every
element of the \(j\)-th part.

For a division \(D\), \(\mathrm{part}(D)\) is this ordered family of parts.
Thus \(\mathrm{part}(D)(i)\) is the \(i\)-th interval part.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def Division (n k : ℕ) : Type :=
  { parts : Fin k → Finset (Fin n) //
    (∀ i, (parts i).Nonempty) ∧
    (∀ ⦃i j : Fin k⦄, i ≠ j → Disjoint (parts i) (parts j)) ∧
    (∀ x : Fin n, ∃ i : Fin k, x ∈ parts i) ∧
    (∀ i ⦃a b c : Fin n⦄,
      a ∈ parts i → c ∈ parts i → a ≤ b → b ≤ c → b ∈ parts i) ∧
    (∀ ⦃i j : Fin k⦄, i < j →
      ∀ ⦃a b : Fin n⦄, a ∈ parts i → b ∈ parts j → a < b) }

/-- The ordered family of parts of an interval division. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.IntervalDivision.part
statements.Title: Ordered part family of an interval division
statements.InlineTexReference:
For natural numbers \(n\) and \(k\), an interval division
\(\mathrm{Division}(n,k)\) is a family of \(k\) finite subsets of
\(\{0,\ldots,n-1\}\).  The parts are nonempty, pairwise disjoint, cover the
whole interval, are convex in the natural order, and are ordered by their
indices: if \(i<j\), every element of the \(i\)-th part is smaller than every
element of the \(j\)-th part.

For a division \(D\), \(\mathrm{part}(D)\) is this ordered family of parts.
Thus \(\mathrm{part}(D)(i)\) is the \(i\)-th interval part.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def part {n k : ℕ} (D : Division n k) : Fin k → Finset (Fin n) :=
  Subtype.val D

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.Cell.Vertical
statements.Title: Vertical divided matrix cell
statements.InlineTexReference:
For a matrix \(M\), a row division \(R\), and a column division \(C\), the
cell \((i,j)\) is vertical if every column of that cell is constant on the
row part \(R_i\).  It is horizontal if every row of that cell is constant on
the column part \(C_j\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def Vertical {n m k : ℕ} (M : _root_.Matrix (Fin n) (Fin m) α)
    (R : IntervalDivision.Division n k) (C : IntervalDivision.Division m k)
    (i j : Fin k) : Prop :=
  ∀ ⦃r₁ r₂ : Fin n⦄,
    r₁ ∈ IntervalDivision.part R i → r₂ ∈ IntervalDivision.part R i →
    ∀ ⦃c : Fin m⦄, c ∈ IntervalDivision.part C j → M r₁ c = M r₂ c

/-- A divided matrix cell is horizontal when each of its rows is constant
inside the column part. -/

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.Cell.Horizontal
statements.Title: Horizontal divided matrix cell
statements.InlineTexReference:
For a matrix \(M\), a row division \(R\), and a column division \(C\), the
cell \((i,j)\) is vertical if every column of that cell is constant on the
row part \(R_i\).  It is horizontal if every row of that cell is constant on
the column part \(C_j\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def Horizontal {n m k : ℕ} (M : _root_.Matrix (Fin n) (Fin m) α)
    (R : IntervalDivision.Division n k) (C : IntervalDivision.Division m k)
    (i j : Fin k) : Prop :=
  ∀ ⦃r : Fin n⦄, r ∈ IntervalDivision.part R i →
    ∀ ⦃c₁ c₂ : Fin m⦄,
      c₁ ∈ IntervalDivision.part C j → c₂ ∈ IntervalDivision.part C j →
      M r c₁ = M r c₂

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.MixedCell.Mixed
statements.Title: Mixed divided matrix cell
statements.InlineTexReference:
For a divided matrix, a cell is mixed if it is neither vertical nor
horizontal.  Equivalently, the cell is not column-wise constant on the row
part and is not row-wise constant on the column part.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def Mixed {n m k : ℕ} (M : _root_.Matrix (Fin n) (Fin m) α)
    (R : IntervalDivision.Division n k) (C : IntervalDivision.Division m k)
    (i j : Fin k) : Prop :=
  ¬ Cell.Vertical M R C i j ∧ ¬ Cell.Horizontal M R C i j

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.MixedMinor.HasMixedMinor
statements.Title: Matrix has a mixed minor
statements.InlineTexReference:
The matrix \(M\) has a \(k\)-mixed minor if \(k=0\), or if there are row and
column \(k\)-divisions such that every one of the \(k^2\) cells is mixed.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def HasMixedMinor {n m : ℕ} (M : _root_.Matrix (Fin n) (Fin m) α) (k : ℕ) :
    Prop :=
  k = 0 ∨
    ∃ R : IntervalDivision.Division n k,
      ∃ C : IntervalDivision.Division m k,
        ∀ i j : Fin k, MixedCell.Mixed M R C i j

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.MixedNumber.matrixMixedNumber
statements.Title: Matrix mixed number
statements.InlineTexReference:
The matrix mixed number of an \(n\times m\) matrix \(M\) is the largest
integer \(k\le \min(n,m)\) for which \(M\) has a \(k\)-mixed minor.

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.VertexOrder.Order
statements.Title: Vertex order
statements.InlineTexReference:
A vertex order of length \(n\) for a vertex type \(V\) is an equivalence
\(\{0,\ldots,n-1\}\simeq V\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def Order (V : Type u) (n : ℕ) : Type u :=
  Fin n ≃ V

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.OrderedAdjacency.orderedAdjacency
statements.Title: Ordered adjacency matrix
statements.InlineTexReference:
Given a graph \(G\) with decidable adjacency and a vertex order \(\sigma\),
the ordered adjacency matrix has Boolean entry \((i,j)\) equal to the truth
value of adjacency between \(\sigma(i)\) and \(\sigma(j)\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def orderedAdjacency {V : Type*} {n : ℕ}
    (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (σ : VertexOrder.Order V n) :
    _root_.Matrix (Fin n) (Fin n) Bool :=
  fun i j => decide (G.Adj ((show Fin n ≃ V from σ) i) ((show Fin n ≃ V from σ) j))

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Matrix.OrderedAdjacencyMixedNumber.orderedAdjacencyMixedNumber
statements.Title: Mixed number of an ordered adjacency matrix
statements.InlineTexReference:
The ordered adjacency mixed number of a graph with a chosen vertex order is
the matrix mixed number of its ordered adjacency matrix.

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.SimpleGraph.mixedMinorNumber
statements.Title: Graph mixed minor number
statements.InlineTexReference:
The graph mixed minor number of a finite graph \(G\) with decidable adjacency
is the infimum, in \(\mathbb{N}\), of the set of natural numbers \(k\) for
which there exists a vertex order \(\sigma\) of length \(|V(G)|\) whose
ordered adjacency mixed number is \(k\).
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
noncomputable def mixedMinorNumber {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : _root_.SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
  sInf { k : ℕ |
    ∃ σ : VertexOrder.Order V (Fintype.card V),
      Matrix.OrderedAdjacencyMixedNumber.orderedAdjacencyMixedNumber G σ = k }

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.GraphParameter.GraphParam
statements.Title: Finite simple-graph parameter
statements.InlineTexReference:
A finite simple-graph parameter assigns a natural number to every finite
simple graph together with a decidable adjacency relation.

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.FunctionalEquivalence.FunctionallyEquivalent
statements.Title: Functional equivalence of graph parameters
statements.InlineTexReference:
Two finite simple-graph parameters \(p\) and \(q\) are functionally
equivalent if there are functions \(f,g:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) such that,
for every finite simple graph \(G\) with decidable adjacency,
\[
  p(G)\le f(q(G))
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  q(G)\le g(p(G)).
\]
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
def FunctionallyEquivalent (p q : GraphParameter.GraphParam) : Prop :=
  (∃ f : ℕ → ℕ, ∀ {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    p (V := V) G ≤ f (q (V := V) G)) ∧
  (∃ g : ℕ → ℕ, ∀ {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    q (V := V) G ≤ g (p (V := V) G))

statements.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Statements.Main.functionalEquivalence
statements.Title: Twin-width and mixed minor number are functionally equivalent
statements.InlineTexReference:
\paragraph{Functional equivalence.}
Let \(\mathrm{twinWidth}\) be the twin-width definition imported from the
Redemption 2 submission, and let \(\mathrm{mixedMinorNumber}\) be the graph
mixed minor number defined in this submission from vertex orders, ordered
adjacency matrices, matrix divisions, mixed cells, mixed minors, and matrix
mixed number.  Both graph parameters are evaluated on finite simple graphs
with decidable adjacency.  The submitted theorem states
\[
  \mathrm{FunctionallyEquivalent}(\mathrm{twinWidth},\mathrm{mixedMinorNumber}),
\]
where \(\mathrm{FunctionallyEquivalent}\) is the submitted graph-parameter
relation saying that each parameter is bounded by a numerical function of the
other.
statements.InlineLeanStatement:
axiom functionalEquivalence :
    FunctionalEquivalence.FunctionallyEquivalent
      (fun {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
        (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj] =>
        TwinWidth.twinWidth G)
      (fun {V : Type} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
        (G : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel G.Adj] =>
        SimpleGraph.mixedMinorNumber G)

proofs.Name: TwinWidthMixedMinorNumberEquivalenceRedemption.Proofs.Main.functionalEquivalence
